abstract: Modern protest movements, from the Arab Spring to contemporary European and Russian unrest, are often characterized as "Twitter revolutions" due to the central role of real-time digital media and international news coverage in shaping mutual reactions across borders. This volume argues that a comparable media revolution occurred in the early modern period with the dissemination of print. This shift fundamentally altered the nature of early modern revolts, which frequently escalated into full-scale propaganda wars once insurgents gained access to the press. Beyond the battle for public opinion, the rise of print deeply impacted how these events were narrated. While domestic governments strictly policed dissident views on internal instability, foreign observers—unburdened by local loyalties—were far freer to analyze and reflect upon these crises. By examining cross-border representations, this book explores how the transnational gaze shaped the historical memory and political reality of early modern revolts.
